1:
"Now these are the commandments, the statutes, and the judgments, which the LORD your God commanded to teach you, that ye might do them in the land whither ye go to possess it:" The witnesses note Moses introducing the commandments, statutes, and judgments to be taught and done in the land. The witnesses note that Gill notes these are not the ten commandments repeated in the preceding chapter, but all others, moral, ceremonial, and judicial, a fresh declaration of the laws he had received. The witnesses note that they were given to teach the people, that they might do them in the land. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the commandments — statutes and judgments; the commanded to teach — the law made known; the that ye might do them — obedience the end of teaching; the in the land — where they were to live them.

The witnesses agree the verse opens the great exposition of the law with its proper end — not knowledge only but obedience: the commandments were "commanded to teach you, that ye might do them," the teaching aimed at the doing, in the land they were about to possess; the joining of instruction and practice shows that God's law is given to be lived, not merely learned, and that the right end of all teaching of his word is a life of obedience in the place of his blessing.

So the witnesses make the verse the law to be done: The witnesses note the the commandments: statutes and judgments. The witnesses note the commanded to teach: the law made known. The witnesses note the that ye might do them: obedience the end of teaching. The witnesses note the in the land: where they were to live them. The expositors observe the the commandments: statutes and judgments. The expositors observe the commanded to teach: the law made known. The expositors observe the that ye might do them: obedience the end of teaching. The expositors observe the in the land: where they were to live them. The believer is taught to mark the the commandments, the commanded to teach, the that ye might do them, the in the land, and to love the LORD with all the heart. The verse thus records that Moses set forth the commandments to be taught and done in the land — God's law given to be lived, the teaching aimed at obedience in the place of his blessing.
2:
"That thou mightest fear the LORD thy God, to keep all his statutes and his commandments... all the days of thy life; and that thy days may be prolonged." The witnesses note the end of the law — that they might fear the LORD all their days, and their days be prolonged. The witnesses note that Gill notes the fear meant is not slavish but filial, a reverential affection for God, as of children to their Father. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the keeping of his statutes all the days of life. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the that thou mightest fear — the end of the law; the a filial fear — reverence, not slavish dread; the all the days of thy life — the abiding temper of the heart; the the root of obedience — the beginning of wisdom (Prov. 1:7).

The witnesses agree the fear of God here commended is the fear of love, not of dread: a "filial" reverence, the affection of children toward their Father, which keeps his commandments out of love and not terror; this fear, maintained all the days of life, is the spring of obedience and the way of long blessing, the holy reverence that honours God as a child honours a father — the beginning of wisdom and the root of a godly life (cf. Prov. 1:7).

So the witnesses make the verse the fear of the LORD: The witnesses note the that thou mightest fear: the end of the law. The witnesses note the a filial fear: reverence, not slavish dread. The witnesses note the all the days of thy life: the abiding temper of the heart. The witnesses note the the root of obedience: the beginning of wisdom (Prov. 1:7). The expositors observe the that thou mightest fear: the end of the law. The expositors observe the a filial fear: reverence, not slavish dread. The expositors observe the all the days of thy life: the abiding temper of the heart. The expositors observe the the root of obedience: the beginning of wisdom (Prov. 1:7). The believer is taught to mark the that thou mightest fear, the a filial fear, the all the days of thy life, the the root of obedience, and to love the LORD with all the heart. The verse thus records that the law's end was that they might fear the LORD all their days — a filial reverence, the fear of love that keeps his commandments, the root of obedience and the beginning of wisdom (Prov. 1:7).
3:
"Hear therefore, O Israel, and observe to do it; that it may be well with thee, and that ye may increase mightily... in the land that floweth with milk and honey." The witnesses note the call to hear and do, that it might be well with them and they increase in the good land. The witnesses note that Gill notes the charge to hear and observe to do the commandments given them. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note the maintenance of the fear of God would bring well-being and mighty increase in the land of milk and honey. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the hear therefore — the call to attention; the observe to do it — hearing made good in doing; the that it may be well — the promise on obedience; the increase mightily — blessing in the land of milk and honey.

The witnesses agree the call binds hearing to doing and promises blessing on obedience: Israel was to "hear... and observe to do," that it might go well with them and they multiply in the fruitful land; the joining of the promise to the precept shows that God's commands are not grievous but the way of welfare, obedience the path to His blessing, the keeping of His word the means by which a people prospers under His favour — hearing made good in doing, and doing crowned with good.

So the witnesses make the verse hear and do: The witnesses note the hear therefore: the call to attention. The witnesses note the observe to do it: hearing made good in doing. The witnesses note the that it may be well: the promise on obedience. The witnesses note the increase mightily: blessing in the land of milk and honey. The expositors observe the hear therefore: the call to attention. The expositors observe the observe to do it: hearing made good in doing. The expositors observe the that it may be well: the promise on obedience. The expositors observe the increase mightily: blessing in the land of milk and honey. The believer is taught to mark the hear therefore, the observe to do it, the that it may be well, the increase mightily, and to love the LORD with all the heart. The verse thus records that Israel was to hear and do, that it might be well with them and they increase — obedience the way of welfare, God's blessing the crown of a people that keeps his word.
4:
"Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God is one LORD:" The witnesses note the great confession of the unity of God — the Shema, Israel's daily creed against idolatry. The witnesses note that Gill notes these are the words of Moses stirring the people to attend to this great and momentous article, the unity of God, to prevent their going into polytheism and idolatry; from this the Jews call the section the Shema, read twice daily. The witnesses note that the verse declares the LORD to be one. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the hear, O Israel — the call to the great confession; the the LORD our God — the one living God; the is one LORD — against all polytheism; the the first commandment — owned by Christ himself (Mark 12:29).

The witnesses agree this verse is the foundation-stone of true religion, the confession of the one living God against all the gods of the heathen: "the LORD our God is one LORD" — the great article that guarded Israel from polytheism and idolatry, and which they were to rehearse morning and evening as their creed; our Lord himself owned it the first of all the commandments (Mark 12:29), the truth of God's oneness underlying the whole law, that the one God alone is to be worshipped and loved with an undivided heart.

So the witnesses make the verse the LORD is one: The witnesses note the hear, O Israel: the call to the great confession. The witnesses note the the LORD our God: the one living God. The witnesses note the is one LORD: against all polytheism. The witnesses note the the first commandment: owned by Christ himself (Mark 12:29). The expositors observe the hear, O Israel: the call to the great confession. The expositors observe the the LORD our God: the one living God. The expositors observe the is one LORD: against all polytheism. The expositors observe the the first commandment: owned by Christ himself (Mark 12:29). The believer is taught to mark the hear, O Israel, the the LORD our God, the is one LORD, the the first commandment, and to love the LORD with all the heart. The verse thus records that the LORD our God is one LORD — the foundation of true religion, the confession against all idolatry, owned by Christ as the first of the commandments (Mark 12:29).
5:
"And thou shalt love the LORD thy God with all thine heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy might." The witnesses note the first and great commandment — to love God with the whole heart, soul, and might. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is the first and chief commandment, the sum and substance of the first table; it includes knowledge of God, esteem, delight, faith, fear, worship, and obedience, which when right springs from it. The witnesses note that God is to be loved for the perfections of his being. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the love the LORD — the first and great command; the with all thine heart — the spring of all service; the soul and might — the whole man claimed; the the root of all — obedience springing from love (Matt. 22:37).

The witnesses agree this is the heart of all religion and the sum of the first table of the law: to love the one God with the whole heart, soul, and might — a love that is no mere feeling but the spring of all true knowledge, faith, fear, worship, and obedience; our Lord named it "the first and great commandment" (Matt. 22:37), for love to God is the root from which all acceptable service grows, and the whole man — heart, soul, and strength — is claimed for him who is one, that he be loved with an undivided and entire devotion.

So the witnesses make the verse love God wholly: The witnesses note the love the LORD: the first and great command. The witnesses note the with all thine heart: the spring of all service. The witnesses note the soul and might: the whole man claimed. The witnesses note the the root of all: obedience springing from love (Matt. 22:37). The expositors observe the love the LORD: the first and great command. The expositors observe the with all thine heart: the spring of all service. The expositors observe the soul and might: the whole man claimed. The expositors observe the the root of all: obedience springing from love (Matt. 22:37). The believer is taught to mark the love the LORD, the with all thine heart, the soul and might, the the root of all, and to love the LORD with all the heart. The verse thus records that Israel was to love the LORD with all the heart, soul, and might — the first and great commandment, love the root from which all acceptable service grows, the whole man claimed for the one God (Matt. 22:37).
6:
"And these words, which I command thee this day, shall be in thine heart:" The witnesses note that the commandments were to be laid up in the heart, not merely heard. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were to hearken to, observe, and take notice that God is one and to be loved in the strongest manner. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note that for the love of God to be right, his commandments must be laid to heart. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the these words — the commandments of God; the shall be in thine heart — laid up within, not on the ear only; the the seat of religion — love springing from the heart; the the law written within — fulfilled in the new covenant (Jer. 31:33).

The witnesses agree the seat of true religion is the heart: the words of God were not to rest on the ear or the tongue only but to dwell "in thine heart," for only a love and obedience that spring from within are real; Keil and Delitzsch mark that the love of God must be of the right kind, rooted in his word laid up within — a truth fulfilled in the new covenant, where God writes his law in the heart (Jer. 31:33), that obedience flow from an inward principle and not an outward constraint.

So the witnesses make the verse in thine heart: The witnesses note the these words: the commandments of God. The witnesses note the shall be in thine heart: laid up within, not on the ear only. The witnesses note the the seat of religion: love springing from the heart. The witnesses note the the law written within: fulfilled in the new covenant (Jer. 31:33). The expositors observe the these words: the commandments of God. The expositors observe the shall be in thine heart: laid up within, not on the ear only. The expositors observe the the seat of religion: love springing from the heart. The expositors observe the the law written within: fulfilled in the new covenant (Jer. 31:33). The believer is taught to mark the these words, the shall be in thine heart, the the seat of religion, the the law written within, and to love the LORD with all the heart. The verse thus records that God's words were to be in the heart — the seat of true religion within, fulfilled in the new covenant where God writes his law in the heart (Jer. 31:33).
7:
"And thou shalt teach them diligently unto thy children, and shalt talk of them when thou sittest in thine house, and when thou walkest by the way, and when thou liest down, and when thou risest up." The witnesses note the charge to teach God's words diligently to children, at all times and in all places. The witnesses note that Gill notes care and pains are to be used to instruct children early in the knowledge of God and his commandments; the word may be rendered "thou shalt whet or sharpen them," to impress them keenly. The witnesses note that the words were to be talked of at home and abroad, lying down and rising up. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the teach diligently — whetting God's words upon them; the unto thy children — the young instructed early; the at all times — sitting, walking, lying down, rising; the religion filling life — the faith handed down (Eph. 6:4).

The witnesses agree the duty of teaching the young is pressed with great earnestness: parents were to "teach diligently" — to whet and sharpen God's words upon their children, and to speak of them at every time and in every place, sitting, walking, lying down, rising up; the all-pervading instruction shows that religion is to fill the whole of life and to be passed on from age to age, the family the first school of godliness, the faith handed down by diligent and constant teaching (cf. Eph. 6:4).

So the witnesses make the verse teach the children: The witnesses note the teach diligently: whetting God's words upon them. The witnesses note the unto thy children: the young instructed early. The witnesses note the at all times: sitting, walking, lying down, rising. The witnesses note the religion filling life: the faith handed down (Eph. 6:4). The expositors observe the teach diligently: whetting God's words upon them. The expositors observe the unto thy children: the young instructed early. The expositors observe the at all times: sitting, walking, lying down, rising. The expositors observe the religion filling life: the faith handed down (Eph. 6:4). The believer is taught to mark the teach diligently, the unto thy children, the at all times, the religion filling life, and to love the LORD with all the heart. The verse thus records that God's words were to be taught diligently to the children at all times — religion filling the whole of life, the family the first school of godliness, the faith handed from age to age (Eph. 6:4).
8:
"And thou shalt bind them for a sign upon thine hand, and they shall be as frontlets between thine eyes." The witnesses note the words to be bound on the hand and worn as frontlets — a constant reminder. The witnesses note that Gill notes that as a man ties anything to his hand for a token to remember it, so were these words to be kept in mind; the Jews understand it literally of binding scrolls (phylacteries) on the hand. The witnesses note that Clarke likewise treats the binding upon the hand. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the bind them on thine hand — a token to remember; the frontlets between the eyes — ever before the sight; the constant remembrance — the word never forgotten; the the life governed — hand and eyes ordered by God's word.

The witnesses agree the figure presses the constant remembrance of God's law: bound on the hand and set before the eyes, the words were to be ever in mind and ever guiding the actions, as a token tied on to keep a thing from being forgotten; whether the Jews' literal phylacteries truly answered the intent or not, the heart of the command is that God's word should govern the hand that works and the eyes that look, the whole life ordered by it — a remembrance not of leather and parchment but of a heart that keeps his word always before it.

So the witnesses make the verse bound and worn: The witnesses note the bind them on thine hand: a token to remember. The witnesses note the frontlets between the eyes: ever before the sight. The witnesses note the constant remembrance: the word never forgotten. The witnesses note the the life governed: hand and eyes ordered by God's word. The expositors observe the bind them on thine hand: a token to remember. The expositors observe the frontlets between the eyes: ever before the sight. The expositors observe the constant remembrance: the word never forgotten. The expositors observe the the life governed: hand and eyes ordered by God's word. The believer is taught to mark the bind them on thine hand, the frontlets between the eyes, the constant remembrance, the the life governed, and to love the LORD with all the heart. The verse thus records that the words were to be bound on the hand and worn as frontlets — a constant remembrance, God's word governing the hand that works and the eyes that look, the whole life ordered by it.
9:
"And thou shalt write them upon the posts of thy house, and on thy gates." The witnesses note God's words to be written on the doorposts and gates — meeting the eye at every coming and going. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was to put them in mind when they went out and came in, that they might observe them; the Jews take it literally, writing the section on a scroll (the mezuzah) and fixing it on the posts. The witnesses note that it served as a continual reminder. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the write them on the posts — of the house; the and on thy gates — at every entrance; the going out and coming in — met at every step; the the home marked for God — consecrated to his word.

The witnesses agree the writing on the posts and gates set God's word in the very path of daily life: it met the eye at every going out and coming in, that the household be ever reminded to observe it; whether by the literal scroll on the doorpost or by the spirit of the command, the intent is that the home itself be marked for God and his word kept in remembrance there, the place of dwelling consecrated to him and the family's every coming and going done under the eye of his law.

So the witnesses make the verse on the posts and gates: The witnesses note the write them on the posts: of the house. The witnesses note the and on thy gates: at every entrance. The witnesses note the going out and coming in: met at every step. The witnesses note the the home marked for God: consecrated to his word. The expositors observe the write them on the posts: of the house. The expositors observe the and on thy gates: at every entrance. The expositors observe the going out and coming in: met at every step. The expositors observe the the home marked for God: consecrated to his word. The believer is taught to mark the write them on the posts, the and on thy gates, the going out and coming in, the the home marked for God, and to love the LORD with all the heart. The verse thus records that God's words were to be written on the posts and gates — meeting the eye at every coming and going, the home itself marked for God and his word kept in remembrance there.
10:
"And it shall be, when the LORD thy God shall have brought thee into the land... great and goodly cities, which thou buildedst not," The witnesses note the warning set against the coming prosperity — cities and goods they had not laboured for. The witnesses note that Gill notes the land of Canaan, on whose borders they now were, with its great cities. The witnesses note that Calvin notes that since wealth and prosperity for the most part blind men's minds, so that they grow wanton and forget moderation, Moses warns them beforehand. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the brought into the land — the promise about to be kept; the cities thou buildedst not — an abundance unearned; the prosperity blinds — wealth making men wanton; the the snare foreseen — the heart to be guarded in plenty.

The reformed reader (Calvin) marks the peril hidden in the promised plenty: God would give Israel great cities they had not built, an abundance they had not earned, and just there lay the danger, for "wealth and prosperity for the most part blind men's minds," making them wanton and forgetful of God; the warning, set before the blessing was received, teaches that prosperity is a subtle snare, and that the heart must be guarded most carefully when outward things are most full, lest the gifts crowd out the Giver.

So the witnesses make the verse the coming plenty: The witnesses note the brought into the land: the promise about to be kept. The witnesses note the cities thou buildedst not: an abundance unearned. The witnesses note the prosperity blinds: wealth making men wanton. The witnesses note the the snare foreseen: the heart to be guarded in plenty. The expositors observe the brought into the land: the promise about to be kept. The expositors observe the cities thou buildedst not: an abundance unearned. The expositors observe the prosperity blinds: wealth making men wanton. The expositors observe the the snare foreseen: the heart to be guarded in plenty. The believer is taught to mark the brought into the land, the cities thou buildedst not, the prosperity blinds, the the snare foreseen, and to love the LORD with all the heart. The verse thus records that God would bring them into a land of cities they had not built — the warning that prosperity is a subtle snare, the heart to be guarded most when outward things are most full, lest the gifts crowd out the Giver.
11:
"And houses full of all good things, which thou filledst not, and wells digged, which thou diggedst not, vineyards and olive trees, which thou plantedst not; when thou shalt have eaten and be full;" The witnesses note the fulness of good things they would receive without their own labour. The witnesses note that Gill notes houses full of good furniture and the fruits of the earth, corn, wine, oil, perhaps gold and silver. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the wells, vineyards, and olive trees they had not made. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the houses full of good — which thou filledst not; the wells, vineyards, olives — which thou madest not; the God's lavish goodness — all from his hand; the a test of the heart — the gift a call to thankfulness (James 1:17).

The witnesses agree the heaping-up of unearned blessings underlines both God's lavish goodness and the testing it brings: full houses, dug wells, planted vineyards — all received from God's hand without their own toil; the very freeness of the gift, meant to draw out gratitude, could harden into presumption if forgotten, so that the abundance of God's mercies becomes a test of the heart, whether it will own the Giver in the gifts or be filled and forget him — every good thing a call to thankful remembrance (cf. James 1:17).

So the witnesses make the verse the unearned fulness: The witnesses note the houses full of good: which thou filledst not. The witnesses note the wells, vineyards, olives: which thou madest not. The witnesses note the God's lavish goodness: all from his hand. The witnesses note the a test of the heart: the gift a call to thankfulness (James 1:17). The expositors observe the houses full of good: which thou filledst not. The expositors observe the wells, vineyards, olives: which thou madest not. The expositors observe the God's lavish goodness: all from his hand. The expositors observe the a test of the heart: the gift a call to thankfulness (James 1:17). The believer is taught to mark the houses full of good, the wells, vineyards, olives, the God's lavish goodness, the a test of the heart, and to love the LORD with all the heart. The verse thus records that Israel would receive houses, wells, and vineyards they had not made — God's lavish goodness a test of the heart, every good gift a call to thankful remembrance of the Giver (James 1:17).
12:
"Then beware lest thou forget the LORD, which brought thee forth out of the land of Egypt, from the house of bondage." The witnesses note the warning at the heart of the chapter — beware of forgetting the LORD amid plenty. The witnesses note that Gill notes creature enjoyments are apt to get possession of the heart and its affections, so beware of forgetting God. The witnesses note that Clarke notes that in earthly prosperity men are apt to forget heavenly things, while the senses have all they wish. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the beware — the great warning; the lest thou forget the LORD — amid the plenty; the who brought thee from Egypt — the house of bondage; the prosperity dulls the soul — the mercies an occasion of forgetting.

The witnesses agree this is the great peril against which the chapter warns: that the heart, filled with earthly good, should "forget the LORD" who gave it and who redeemed them from bondage; Clarke marks how prosperity dulls the soul to heavenly things, the senses satisfied and God neglected — a danger as real for the believer as for Israel, that the very mercies of God become the occasion of forgetting him, against which the only safeguard is a heart that holds fast its Redeemer in the day of plenty as in the day of need.

So the witnesses make the verse beware lest thou forget: The witnesses note the beware: the great warning. The witnesses note the lest thou forget the LORD: amid the plenty. The witnesses note the who brought thee from Egypt: the house of bondage. The witnesses note the prosperity dulls the soul: the mercies an occasion of forgetting. The expositors observe the beware: the great warning. The expositors observe the lest thou forget the LORD: amid the plenty. The expositors observe the who brought thee from Egypt: the house of bondage. The expositors observe the prosperity dulls the soul: the mercies an occasion of forgetting. The believer is taught to mark the beware, the lest thou forget the LORD, the who brought thee from Egypt, the prosperity dulls the soul, and to love the LORD with all the heart. The verse thus records that Israel was to beware lest they forget the LORD who redeemed them — the great peril of plenty, that God's mercies become the occasion of forgetting him, guarded only by a heart that holds fast its Redeemer.
13:
"Thou shalt fear the LORD thy God, and serve him, and shalt swear by his name." The witnesses note the positive charge — to fear, serve, and swear by the LORD alone, a word Christ used against Satan. The witnesses note that Gill notes the fear is filial, a reverential affection for the God who brought them from bondage into liberty and plenty; they should fear and serve him. The witnesses note that Clarke notes this is one of the passages Christ used to repel the temptations of Satan (Matt. 4:10). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the fear the LORD — with filial reverence; the and serve him — him alone; the swear by his name — owning him the one God; the Christ's answer to Satan — him only shalt thou serve (Matt. 4:10).

The witnesses agree the verse gathers the whole duty of man toward God into three: to fear him with filial reverence, to serve him alone, and to swear by his name only — owning him as the one God in worship, obedience, and oath; our Lord wielded this very word against the tempter — "thou shalt worship the Lord thy God, and him only shalt thou serve" (Matt. 4:10) — making it the believer's charter against every rival claim, that the one God is to be feared, served, and confessed with an undivided allegiance.

So the witnesses make the verse fear and serve him: The witnesses note the fear the LORD: with filial reverence. The witnesses note the and serve him: him alone. The witnesses note the swear by his name: owning him the one God. The witnesses note the Christ's answer to Satan: him only shalt thou serve (Matt. 4:10). The expositors observe the fear the LORD: with filial reverence. The expositors observe the and serve him: him alone. The expositors observe the swear by his name: owning him the one God. The expositors observe the Christ's answer to Satan: him only shalt thou serve (Matt. 4:10). The believer is taught to mark the fear the LORD, the and serve him, the swear by his name, the Christ's answer to Satan, and to love the LORD with all the heart. The verse thus records that Israel was to fear, serve, and swear by the LORD alone — the whole duty toward God, the word Christ used against the tempter, "him only shalt thou serve" (Matt. 4:10).
14:
"Ye shall not go after other gods, of the gods of the people which are round about you;" The witnesses note the prohibition of idolatry — not to follow the gods of the surrounding nations. The witnesses note that Gill notes to go after other gods is to serve and worship them, and so depart from the true God; these are no gods, only nominal and fictitious, idols that are nothing in the world. The witnesses note that Clarke likewise warns against following other gods. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the go not after other gods — no following the heathen; the of the people round about — the idols of the nations; the nothing in the world — mere fictions, no true gods; the the undivided heart — no rival beside the one LORD.

The witnesses agree the command flows straight from the unity of God confessed in the Shema: since the LORD is one, Israel must follow no other gods, the idols of the nations being "nothing in the world," mere fictions unworthy of any worship; to go after them is to forsake the true God and play the harlot from him, and so the love commanded toward the one God carries with it the absolute refusal of all rivals — an undivided heart admitting no idol beside the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse no other gods: The witnesses note the go not after other gods: no following the heathen. The witnesses note the of the people round about: the idols of the nations. The witnesses note the nothing in the world: mere fictions, no true gods. The witnesses note the the undivided heart: no rival beside the one LORD. The expositors observe the go not after other gods: no following the heathen. The expositors observe the of the people round about: the idols of the nations. The expositors observe the nothing in the world: mere fictions, no true gods. The expositors observe the the undivided heart: no rival beside the one LORD. The believer is taught to mark the go not after other gods, the of the people round about, the nothing in the world, the the undivided heart, and to serve the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that Israel was to follow no other gods — the command flowing from the unity of God, the idols nothing in the world, the love of the one LORD admitting no rival.
15:
"(For the LORD thy God is a jealous God among you) lest the anger of the LORD thy God be kindled against thee, and destroy thee from off the face of the earth." The witnesses note the ground of the prohibition — the LORD is a jealous God, whose anger idolatry would kindle. The witnesses note that Gill notes God was near them, in the midst of them, his tabernacle between their camps; a God jealous of his honour in worship, who would resent any affront. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the warning lest his anger be kindled. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a jealous God — in the midst of his people; the jealous of his honour — sharing his glory with no idol; the his anger kindled — idolatry provoking him; the holy zeal — the husband's claim on an exclusive love (Exod. 34:14).

The witnesses agree the jealousy of God is the solemn guard upon the first commandment: dwelling in the midst of his people, the LORD is jealous for his honour and will not share his glory with idols, so that to go after other gods would kindle his anger to their destruction; the divine jealousy is no petty passion but the holy zeal of a husband for an exclusive love, that the people bound to him in covenant give him their whole heart — a warning that idolatry is spiritual adultery, provoking the God who claims his own (cf. Exod. 34:14).

So the witnesses make the verse a jealous God: The witnesses note the a jealous God: in the midst of his people. The witnesses note the jealous of his honour: sharing his glory with no idol. The witnesses note the his anger kindled: idolatry provoking him. The witnesses note the holy zeal: the husband's claim on an exclusive love (Exod. 34:14). The expositors observe the a jealous God: in the midst of his people. The expositors observe the jealous of his honour: sharing his glory with no idol. The expositors observe the his anger kindled: idolatry provoking him. The expositors observe the holy zeal: the husband's claim on an exclusive love (Exod. 34:14). The believer is taught to mark the a jealous God, the jealous of his honour, the his anger kindled, the holy zeal, and to serve the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that the LORD is a jealous God whose anger idolatry would kindle — his holy zeal claiming the whole heart, idolatry a spiritual adultery against the God who dwells among his own (Exod. 34:14).
16:
"Ye shall not tempt the LORD your God, as ye tempted him in Massah." The witnesses note the charge not to tempt God by murmuring or unbelief, as at Massah — a word Christ used against Satan. The witnesses note that Gill notes to tempt God is to strive against him, murmur at his providences, or require a sign of him; this is another passage Christ used to repel Satan. The witnesses note that Clarke notes they were not to provoke him by entertaining doubts of his presence or care. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the tempt not — put not God to the proof; the as in Massah — the old murmuring unbelief; the requiring a sign — distrust of his care; the Christ's answer — thou shalt not tempt the Lord (Matt. 4:7).

The witnesses agree the command forbids the unbelief that puts God to the proof: to "tempt" him, as Israel did at Massah demanding water and doubting his presence, is to distrust his care and provoke him by requiring fresh signs of what he has already promised; our Lord met the tempter with this very word — "thou shalt not tempt the Lord thy God" (Matt. 4:7) — refusing to cast himself down to force a proof of God's care, and teaching the believer to trust God's word without demanding signs, the opposite of faith being this proud testing of God.

So the witnesses make the verse tempt not the LORD: The witnesses note the tempt not: put not God to the proof. The witnesses note the as in Massah: the old murmuring unbelief. The witnesses note the requiring a sign: distrust of his care. The witnesses note the Christ's answer: thou shalt not tempt the Lord (Matt. 4:7). The expositors observe the tempt not: put not God to the proof. The expositors observe the as in Massah: the old murmuring unbelief. The expositors observe the requiring a sign: distrust of his care. The expositors observe the Christ's answer: thou shalt not tempt the Lord (Matt. 4:7). The believer is taught to mark the tempt not, the as in Massah, the requiring a sign, the Christ's answer, and to serve the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that Israel was not to tempt the LORD as at Massah — the unbelief that distrusts his care and demands fresh signs, met by Christ against Satan, "thou shalt not tempt the Lord thy God" (Matt. 4:7).
17:
"Ye shall diligently keep the commandments of the LORD your God, and his testimonies, and his statutes, which he hath commanded thee." The witnesses note the charge to keep God's commandments with diligence — religion requiring care and pains. The witnesses note that Gill notes not only the ten commands but all others were to be kept. The witnesses note that Henry notes it requires a great deal of care and pains to keep up religion in the power of it in our hearts and lives, negligence being its undoing. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the diligently keep — with care and pains; the the commandments — testimonies and statutes; the religion's power — soon lost through negligence; the earnest obedience — love proved in careful keeping.

The puritan expositor (Henry) presses the diligence the duty demands: to keep God's commandments is no light or careless thing but requires "care and pains," for religion in its power is soon lost through negligence; the heart and life are kept faithful only by watchful effort, the commandments diligently observed, lest the soul slip into formality or decay — a call to earnest, painstaking obedience, that the love of God confessed be proved in the careful keeping of his word.

So the witnesses make the verse diligently keep: The witnesses note the diligently keep: with care and pains. The witnesses note the the commandments: testimonies and statutes. The witnesses note the religion's power: soon lost through negligence. The witnesses note the earnest obedience: love proved in careful keeping. The expositors observe the diligently keep: with care and pains. The expositors observe the the commandments: testimonies and statutes. The expositors observe the religion's power: soon lost through negligence. The expositors observe the earnest obedience: love proved in careful keeping. The believer is taught to mark the diligently keep, the the commandments, the religion's power, the earnest obedience, and to serve the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that Israel was diligently to keep God's commandments — religion in its power requiring care and pains, the love of God proved in the watchful, earnest keeping of his word.
18:
"And thou shalt do that which is right and good in the sight of the LORD: that it may be well with thee..." The witnesses note the charge to do what is right and good in God's sight, with the promise of well-being. The witnesses note that Gill notes what is right and good appears from the declaration of God's will in his commandments, which are holy, just, and good; obeying them is doing right, that it may be well. The witnesses note that the promise is added — that it may be well with thee. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the do what is right — defined by God's will; the and good — his commandments holy and just; the in the sight of the LORD — his standard, not man's; the that it may be well — obedience the path of blessing (Rom. 7:12).

The witnesses agree the standard of right is God's own will, and the doing of it the way of welfare: what is "right and good" is not left to man's judgment but defined by God's commandments, which are holy, just, and good, so that to obey them is to do right in his sight; the promise joined to the precept — "that it may be well with thee" — shows that God's way is the good way for man, his commands the path of blessing, the obedient finding it well with them under his favour (cf. Rom. 7:12).

So the witnesses make the verse right and good: The witnesses note the do what is right: defined by God's will. The witnesses note the and good: his commandments holy and just. The witnesses note the in the sight of the LORD: his standard, not man's. The witnesses note the that it may be well: obedience the path of blessing (Rom. 7:12). The expositors observe the do what is right: defined by God's will. The expositors observe the and good: his commandments holy and just. The expositors observe the in the sight of the LORD: his standard, not man's. The expositors observe the that it may be well: obedience the path of blessing (Rom. 7:12). The believer is taught to mark the do what is right, the and good, the in the sight of the LORD, the that it may be well, and to serve the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that Israel was to do what is right and good in God's sight — the standard his own holy will, obedience the way of welfare, his commands the path of blessing (Rom. 7:12).
19:
"To cast out all thine enemies from before thee, as the LORD hath spoken." The witnesses note God's promise, as with an oath, to cast out their enemies before them. The witnesses note that Gill notes the LORD promised, and as it seems with an oath, to drive out their enemies and make way for their settlement in the land. The witnesses note that this was as the LORD had spoken. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the cast out thine enemies — god's own work; the from before thee — making room in the land; the as the LORD hath spoken — promised and sworn; the his faithfulness — the conquest his gift, not their achievement.

The witnesses agree the casting-out of the enemies was God's own work, promised and sworn: he would drive out the nations before Israel and make room for them in the land, the conquest his gift and not their achievement; the joining of this promise to the call to obedience shows the gracious order of God's dealing — that as the people kept his word, he would fulfil his, going before them to subdue their foes, the inheritance secured by his faithfulness to what he had spoken.

So the witnesses make the verse enemies cast out: The witnesses note the cast out thine enemies: God's own work. The witnesses note the from before thee: making room in the land. The witnesses note the as the LORD hath spoken: promised and sworn. The witnesses note the his faithfulness: the conquest his gift, not their achievement. The expositors observe the cast out thine enemies: God's own work. The expositors observe the from before thee: making room in the land. The expositors observe the as the LORD hath spoken: promised and sworn. The expositors observe the his faithfulness: the conquest his gift, not their achievement. The believer is taught to mark the cast out thine enemies, the from before thee, the as the LORD hath spoken, the his faithfulness, and to serve the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that God promised to cast out their enemies before them — the conquest his own work, sworn and sure, the inheritance secured by his faithfulness to what he had spoken.
20:
"And when thy son asketh thee in time to come, saying, What mean the testimonies, and the statutes, and the judgments, which the LORD our God hath commanded you?" The witnesses note the foreseen question of the son, asking the meaning of God's testimonies and statutes. The witnesses note that Gill notes "in time to come," or "tomorrow," meaning in later times, after they came into Canaan and the ordinances took effect. The witnesses note that Clarke, with Ainsworth, marks the place of catechizing children in the faith. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the when thy son asketh — the child's question foreseen; the what mean the testimonies — the meaning of the law; the in time to come — in the days to follow; the faith handed on — taught with its reasons, not by rote.

The witnesses agree the verse provides for the handing-on of the faith through the questions of children: the son would in time ask the meaning of God's commandments, and the parent was to be ready with an answer; the foreseeing of the child's question shows God's care that each generation understand, not merely keep, his statutes — that religion be taught with its reasons, the young led to the spring of the law, that they obey from understanding and not by rote, the faith passed on living from father to son.

So the witnesses make the verse the son's question: The witnesses note the when thy son asketh: the child's question foreseen. The witnesses note the what mean the testimonies: the meaning of the law. The witnesses note the in time to come: in the days to follow. The witnesses note the faith handed on: taught with its reasons, not by rote. The expositors observe the when thy son asketh: the child's question foreseen. The expositors observe the what mean the testimonies: the meaning of the law. The expositors observe the in time to come: in the days to follow. The expositors observe the faith handed on: taught with its reasons, not by rote. The believer is taught to mark the when thy son asketh, the what mean the testimonies, the in time to come, the faith handed on, and to serve the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that the son would ask the meaning of God's statutes — the handing-on of the faith through children's questions, religion taught with its reasons, the young led to understand and not merely keep the law.
21:
"Then thou shalt say unto thy son, We were Pharaoh's bondmen in Egypt; and the LORD brought us out of Egypt with a mighty hand:" The witnesses note the answer to the son — the story of redemption from Egyptian bondage. The witnesses note that Gill notes the answer leads the son into the spring and original of the commandments, and acquaints him with the goodness of God that laid them under obligation to obey. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the reply — we were Pharaoh's bondmen, and the LORD brought us out with a mighty hand. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the we were bondmen — in Egypt under Pharaoh; the the LORD brought us out — with a mighty hand; the the spring of the law — redemption the ground of obedience; the grateful response — obeying because delivered (1 John 4:19).

The witnesses agree the answer grounds all obedience in redemption: the child was to be told that they had been bondmen in Egypt, and the LORD had brought them out "with a mighty hand"; the law is thus explained not as a bare command but as the grateful response of a redeemed people to their Redeemer, the goodness of God in delivering them being the spring and reason of their obedience — a pattern of all true religion, which obeys not to earn deliverance but because it has been delivered (cf. 1 John 4:19).

So the witnesses make the verse the redemption told: The witnesses note the we were bondmen: in Egypt under Pharaoh. The witnesses note the the LORD brought us out: with a mighty hand. The witnesses note the the spring of the law: redemption the ground of obedience. The witnesses note the grateful response: obeying because delivered (1 John 4:19). The expositors observe the we were bondmen: in Egypt under Pharaoh. The expositors observe the the LORD brought us out: with a mighty hand. The expositors observe the the spring of the law: redemption the ground of obedience. The expositors observe the grateful response: obeying because delivered (1 John 4:19). The believer is taught to mark the we were bondmen, the the LORD brought us out, the the spring of the law, the grateful response, and to serve the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that the son was to be told of the redemption from Egypt — the law grounded in deliverance, obedience the grateful response of a redeemed people to their Redeemer (1 John 4:19).
22:
"And the LORD shewed signs and wonders, great and sore, upon Egypt, upon Pharaoh, and upon all his household, before our eyes:" The witnesses note the signs and wonders — the plagues — wrought upon Egypt before their eyes. The witnesses note that Gill notes the ten plagues, signs of the power of God, great and sore, very distressing to the Egyptians. The witnesses note that they came heavy upon Pharaoh and all his household. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the signs and wonders — the ten plagues; the great and sore — a power above nature; the upon Egypt and Pharaoh — the judgments on the oppressor; the before our eyes — the memory fixed for the children.

The witnesses agree the wonders upon Egypt are rehearsed as the proof of God's mighty power in their redemption: the ten plagues, "great and sore," fell upon Pharaoh and his people, signs of a power above nature wrought before Israel's own eyes; the recalling of them to the son fixes in the next generation the memory of what God had done, that they too might know the greatness of their Redeemer and the cost at which they were delivered — the judgments on Egypt the dark backdrop against which God's saving power shone.

So the witnesses make the verse signs and wonders: The witnesses note the signs and wonders: the ten plagues. The witnesses note the great and sore: a power above nature. The witnesses note the upon Egypt and Pharaoh: the judgments on the oppressor. The witnesses note the before our eyes: the memory fixed for the children. The expositors observe the signs and wonders: the ten plagues. The expositors observe the great and sore: a power above nature. The expositors observe the upon Egypt and Pharaoh: the judgments on the oppressor. The expositors observe the before our eyes: the memory fixed for the children. The believer is taught to mark the signs and wonders, the great and sore, the upon Egypt and Pharaoh, the before our eyes, and to serve the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that the LORD showed great signs upon Egypt before their eyes — the wonders of their redemption rehearsed to the children, that they know the greatness of their Redeemer.
23:
"And he brought us out from thence, that he might bring us in, to give us the land which he sware unto our fathers." The witnesses note the purpose of the deliverance — God brought them out that he might bring them in to the promised land. The witnesses note that Gill notes he brought them out of a state of bondage and misery, in order to bring them into the land. The witnesses note that this was the land sworn to their fathers. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the brought us out — from bondage and misery; the that he might bring us in — the destination of redemption; the the land sworn — to the fathers; the he completes his work — bringing out and in (Phil. 1:6).

The witnesses agree the verse states the gracious design of redemption — not deliverance only but a destination: God "brought us out... that he might bring us in," the freeing from Egypt aimed at the bringing into the inheritance sworn to the fathers; the truth that God redeems his people for a purpose, to bring them to the promised good, is the comfort of all the saved — that he who begins the work of salvation will complete it, bringing out from bondage and in to the rest, faithful to his oath (cf. Phil. 1:6).

So the witnesses make the verse out, that he might bring in: The witnesses note the brought us out: from bondage and misery. The witnesses note the that he might bring us in: the destination of redemption. The witnesses note the the land sworn: to the fathers. The witnesses note the he completes his work: bringing out and in (Phil. 1:6). The expositors observe the brought us out: from bondage and misery. The expositors observe the that he might bring us in: the destination of redemption. The expositors observe the the land sworn: to the fathers. The expositors observe the he completes his work: bringing out and in (Phil. 1:6). The believer is taught to mark the brought us out, the that he might bring us in, the the land sworn, the he completes his work, and to serve the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that God brought them out that he might bring them in — redemption with a purpose, the comfort that he who begins the work completes it, faithful to his oath (Phil. 1:6).
24:
"And the LORD commanded us to do all these statutes, to fear the LORD our God, for our good always, that he might preserve us alive, as it is at this day." The witnesses note the statutes commanded for their good — to fear God, for their lasting welfare and preservation. The witnesses note that Gill notes some statutes were designed to commemorate the deliverance, as the passover; all were to be obeyed in gratitude for such favours. The witnesses note that they were commanded for their good always, that God might preserve them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the commanded all these statutes — the law given to keep; the to fear the LORD — the heart of obedience; the for our good always — the law a gift of love; the that he might preserve us — obedience the way of life (Deut. 10:13).

The witnesses agree the law is here shown to be a gift of love, given for the people's good: God commanded the statutes "for our good always," that fearing him they might be preserved and prosper; the obedience required is not a burden but a benefit, the commandments framed for the welfare of those who keep them — so that the law itself is mercy, and the keeping of it the way of life, God seeking in all his commands the lasting good of his redeemed people (cf. Deut. 10:13).

So the witnesses make the verse for our good always: The witnesses note the commanded all these statutes: the law given to keep. The witnesses note the to fear the LORD: the heart of obedience. The witnesses note the for our good always: the law a gift of love. The witnesses note the that he might preserve us: obedience the way of life (Deut. 10:13). The expositors observe the commanded all these statutes: the law given to keep. The expositors observe the to fear the LORD: the heart of obedience. The expositors observe the for our good always: the law a gift of love. The expositors observe the that he might preserve us: obedience the way of life (Deut. 10:13). The believer is taught to mark the commanded all these statutes, the to fear the LORD, the for our good always, the that he might preserve us, and to serve the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that God commanded the statutes for their good always — the law a gift of love, obedience not a burden but a benefit, God seeking in his commands the lasting good of his people (Deut. 10:13).
25:
"And it shall be our righteousness, if we observe to do all these commandments before the LORD our God, as he hath commanded us." The witnesses note that the keeping of all the commandments would be their righteousness — a demand pointing beyond itself. The witnesses note that Gill notes that to have such a justifying righteousness a man must keep all the commandments of God, not one excepted, and that perfectly, without the least failure. The witnesses note that this was to be done before the LORD their God, as commanded. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the it shall be our righteousness — a righteousness by the law; the if we observe to do all — every command, none excepted; the perfectly, without failure — what no fallen man can do; the the schoolmaster to Christ — the law pointing to the righteousness of faith (Gal. 3:24).

The witnesses agree the verse, rightly weighed, drives the soul to the gospel: a righteousness by the law would indeed be theirs "if we observe to do all these commandments" — but, as Gill presses, this requires keeping every command perfectly, without the least failure, which no fallen man can do; the law thus sets a standard it gives no power to reach, and so becomes a schoolmaster to bring us to Christ (Gal. 3:24), in whom alone is the perfect righteousness the law demands — the verse, while urging obedience, pointing past it to the righteousness of faith.

So the witnesses make the verse our righteousness: The witnesses note the it shall be our righteousness: a righteousness by the law. The witnesses note the if we observe to do all: every command, none excepted. The witnesses note the perfectly, without failure: what no fallen man can do. The witnesses note the the schoolmaster to Christ: the law pointing to the righteousness of faith (Gal. 3:24). The expositors observe the it shall be our righteousness: a righteousness by the law. The expositors observe the if we observe to do all: every command, none excepted. The expositors observe the perfectly, without failure: what no fallen man can do. The expositors observe the the schoolmaster to Christ: the law pointing to the righteousness of faith (Gal. 3:24). The believer is taught to mark the it shall be our righteousness, the if we observe to do all, the perfectly, without failure, the the schoolmaster to Christ, and to serve the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that the keeping of all the commandments would be their righteousness — a standard requiring perfect obedience no fallen man can render, the law a schoolmaster pointing past itself to the righteousness of Christ by faith (Gal. 3:24).
